1: A type: The man with the measuring line going to measure Jerusalem for rebuilding is a type of Christ as the builder of the new Jerusalem. 'I will build my church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it' (Matthew 16:18). Christ surveys and builds the spiritual Jerusalem — the church — that is greater than the physical city. The measuring line is the surveyor's tool of the one who builds; Christ is the architect and builder of the heavenly city (Hebrews 11:10: the city whose designer and builder is God).
2: A type: The city to be measured — its width and length — anticipates the measurements of the new Jerusalem in Revelation 21:15-17 (the city measured by an angel with a golden measuring rod, 1500 miles in each direction). The rebuilding survey of Zechariah's vision is the beginning of the process that culminates in the new creation city whose dimensions reflect the fullness of God's glory.
3: A theme: The two angels in urgent dialogue — one going out to measure and one coming to intercept — represent the urgency of the divine plan. Christ's sense of urgency in accomplishing his mission: 'I must work the works of him who sent me while it is day; night is coming when no one can work' (John 9:4). The urgency of the restoration message in Zechariah's vision is the urgency of the incarnation: the moment of divine intervention cannot be delayed.
4: A theme: Jerusalem as unwalled villages overflowing with people — too large to be contained — is the NT's picture of the church's expansion. 'The kingdom of heaven is like leaven that a woman took and hid in three measures of flour, till it was all leavened' (Matthew 13:33). The overflow of the spiritual Jerusalem — the church — exceeds any wall, ethnicity, or boundary: 'There is neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free, male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus' (Galatians 3:28).
5: A direct revelation: 'I will be to her a wall of fire all around... and I will be the glory in her midst.' This is the most concentrated Christological declaration in the first two chapters. Christ is the wall of fire — the divine protection that Paul describes as 'nothing can separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord' (Romans 8:38-39). Christ is the glory in the midst — John's 'the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we have seen his glory' (John 1:14). Both aspects of divine protection and divine presence are fulfilled in the person of the incarnate Son.
6: A theme: The urgent call to flee from Babylon is Christ's call to come out of the world's systems. The NT's 'Come out of her, my people, lest you take part in her sins, lest you share in her plagues' (Revelation 18:4) applies the same Babylon-exit call to the eschatological moment. Christ calls his people out of all that Babylon represents — spiritual complacency, worldly comfort, exile from God — into the freedom of the new Jerusalem.
7: A theme: The call to 'escape to Zion' from 'the daughter of Babylon' is the call to come to Christ. Hebrews presents the Christian life as coming to 'Mount Zion and to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem' (Hebrews 12:22). The choice between Babylon (the world's prosperity, comfort, and safety) and Zion (God's presence, purpose, and future) is the fundamental choice Christ presents: 'You cannot serve God and money' (Matthew 6:24).
8: A direct revelation: 'He who touches you touches the apple of his eye.' Christ identifies himself with his people so fully that what is done to them is done to him: 'Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me?' (Acts 9:4). The intimate identification — Israel as the pupil of YHWH's eye — is Christ's identification with the least of his brethren: 'As you did it to one of the least of these my brothers, you did it to me' (Matthew 25:40). The divine protectiveness toward the covenant community is Christ's own identification with the suffering church.
9: A theme: 'You will know that the LORD of hosts has sent me' — the authenticating formula appears five times in Zechariah's visions. The same authentication criterion applies to Christ: 'The works that I do in my Father's name bear witness about me' (John 10:25). The works of Jesus authenticate his sending: 'If I am not doing the works of my Father, then do not believe me, but if I do them, even though you do not believe me, believe the works' (John 10:37-38).
10: A direct revelation: 'Behold, I am coming and I will dwell in your midst, declares the LORD.' This verse is one of the OT's most direct incarnational prophecies. John 1:14 is the fulfillment: 'The Word became flesh and dwelt (eskēnōsen, tabernacled) among us.' The Greek translation of 'dwell' (shakan in Hebrew) uses the same word as God's tabernacling presence in the wilderness. YHWH's promise to dwell in the midst of Jerusalem is fulfilled when the divine Son takes up residence in human flesh.
11: A direct revelation: 'And many nations shall join themselves to the LORD in that day, and shall be my people.' The Gentile mission — the joining of the nations to YHWH's people — is Christ's explicit commission: 'Go therefore and make disciples of all nations' (Matthew 28:19). Paul's theology of Gentile inclusion (Ephesians 2:11-22: the dividing wall of hostility broken down in Christ) is the fulfillment of this vision. The 'you will know that the LORD of hosts has sent me' is fulfilled when the nations come to faith in Christ.
12: A theme: YHWH inheriting Judah as his portion in the holy land is the extraordinary reversal of the covenant — God as the heir, not just the giver. Christ the Son is the heir of all things: 'In these last days he has spoken to us by his Son, whom he appointed the heir of all things' (Hebrews 1:2). The divine inheritance of the holy land is expanded in Christ to the inheritance of the whole earth: 'Blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth' (Matthew 5:5) — the meek who inherit are those united to Christ the heir.
13: A theme: 'Be silent, all flesh, before the LORD, for he has roused himself from his holy dwelling.' The cosmic silence before the divine act is the silence before the incarnation — 400 years of prophetic silence between Malachi and the forerunner's cry. Christ is the divine word breaking the silence: 'In the beginning was the Word... and the Word became flesh' (John 1:1, 14). The silence of all flesh before YHWH's rousing is the silence of the cosmos before the first cry of the incarnate Son.
